question.text: An importer who needs to buy USD in 3 months creates a zero-cost collar on USDINR with spot at Rs 84: buys Rs 85 call and sells Rs 83 put. What is the effective exchange rate range?

answer.text: The importer's effective buying rate will be between Rs 83 and Rs 85 — below Rs 83, they are obligated to buy at Rs 83 (due to short put); above Rs 85, they buy at Rs 85 (exercise call); between 83-85, they buy at market rate
The effective rate is always Rs 84 regardless of market movement
The importer buys at Rs 85 only if USDINR rises above Rs 85
The collar guarantees the importer can always buy at Rs 83

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Collar creates a band: If USDINR < 83: put is exercised against importer — they must buy at 83 (floor). If 83 ≤ USDINR ≤ 85: both options expire OTM — buy at market rate. If USDINR > 85: call is exercised — buy at 85 (cap). The importer is protected above 85 but gives up benefit below 83. **The Trap:** The rate isn't fixed at 84 — it varies within the 83-85 band. Below 83, the put obligation kicks in. The importer can't always buy at 83 — they must buy at 83 even if the market rate is lower. **Cross-Exam Context:** Currency collars are covered in Series I Ch5. This is the most common hedging structure for importers/exporters in practice. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Zero-cost collars are the most popular corporate hedging tool because they cost nothing upfront — the trade-off is giving up favorable moves beyond the sold option's strike.
